summary.brief: Cole v. Chun. Medical malpractice case involving paediatrics.

evidence_index.text: In June 2011, plaintiff began treatment with defendant Robert A. Eden, an ophthalmologist employed by defendant Cornea Consultants of Albany, PLLC, for a change in his vision and a small white spot in his left eye.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: Eden performed the procedure in August 2011 and, in doing so, placed sutures within plaintiff's left eye that would later require removal.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: Eden began removing the sutures in January 2012, and defendant Adam Chun, a first-year ophthalmology fellow employed by Cornea Consultants, removed additional sutures in July 2012 but had difficulty doing so, which resulted in Chun 'tugging' on plaintiff's eye.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: Plaintiff called Cornea Consultants for an antibiotic prescription and was advised instead to go to the hospital.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: Upon examination the following morning, Chun and Eden observed a cornea ulcer, loose stitches, an area of significant epithelial defect and a corneal melt in plaintiff's left eye.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: Later that day, the transplant 'fell apart' requiring an emergency corneal transplant.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: Plaintiff remains without visual acuity in his left eye and operates with light perception only.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: Supreme Court granted Albany Medical's motion in its entirety, but denied the motion made by Cornea Consultants, finding issues of fact as to both the lack of informed consent and medical malpractice causes of action.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: Cykiert opined, within a reasonable degree of medical certainty, that 'there is absolutely no proximate cause between the alleged departures of . . . Chun and Eden and the injuries alleged by [plaintiff].'
evidence_index.speaker: Expert witness

evidence_index.text: Klein opined, 'within a reasonable degree of medical certainty, that [Cornea Consultants] deviated from good and accepted ophthalmological practice by discharging [plaintiff] on July 31, 2012 without the necessary antibiotics.'
evidence_index.speaker: Expert witness

evidence_index.text: We find that, when '[v]iewing the evidence in a light most favorable to plaintiff'... plaintiff's expert affidavit raises a question of fact as to whether Cornea Consultants' alleged deviation from acceptable medical practice proximately caused plaintiff's injuries.
evidence_index.speaker: Judge's analysis

evidence_index.text: ORDERED that the order is affirmed, with costs.
evidence_index.speaker: Court order

initial_state.chief_complaint: Change in vision and a small white spot in left eye
initial_state.history_of_present_illness: Patient began treatment for vision changes and a white spot in the left eye. Underwent a full thickness corneal transplant in August 2011.

focused_exam.eye: Cornea ulcer, loose stitches, significant epithelial defect, corneal melt observed

requestables.name: Ophthalmology Consultation
requestables.description: Consultation with ophthalmologist for corneal transplant complications

reveal.result_summary: Cornea ulcer, loose stitches, significant epithelial defect, corneal melt
reveal.decision_impact: Requires immediate intervention to prevent further damage

timeline_phases.name: Initial Presentation
timeline_phases.description: Patient presents with vision changes and a white spot in the left eye

key_events.event: Observation of corneal ulcer and other complications

decision_points.prompt: What should be the next step in management?

options.clinical_reasoning: Antibiotics are standard to prevent infection post-suture removal
Failed to prevent infection, leading to severe complications

expected_action_court.standard_of_care_basis: Expert evidence established antibiotics are necessary post-suture removal

explanation.why_defendant_wrong: Failure to administer antibiotics led to infection and severe complications
explanation.what_should_have_happened: Antibiotics should have been given to prevent infection
explanation.legal_standard_applied: Standard of care as established by expert testimony

patient_outcome.description: Patient remains without visual acuity in left eye, operates with light perception only

key_findings: Cornea Consultants failed to establish entitlement to summary judgment
Issues of fact regarding lack of informed consent and medical malpractice

malpractice_determination.point_of_failure: Failure to administer antibiotics post-suture removal

treatments.outcome: Transplant fell apart, requiring emergency intervention

allegations.allegation: Failure to administer antibiotics post-suture removal

court_findings.finding: Cornea Consultants failed to establish entitlement to summary judgment
court_findings.legal_significance: Issues of fact regarding lack of informed consent and medical malpractice
